Evaluation of model performance on the Training Set¶

In [65]:
# Evaluating tuned Random Forest Classifier on training data
random_forest_classifier_tuned_training_perf = model_performance_evaluation(random_forest_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

random_forest_classifier_tuned_training_perf
Out[65]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.762985 0.783672 0.891103 0.833942
In [66]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned Random Forest Classifier on training data
confusion_matrix(random_forest_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

Observations:

  • The tuned Random Forest Classifier achieved a training accuracy of 0.7787, which means the model correctly classified around 77.9 percent of the training records.
  • The precision score is 0.7984, indicating that nearly 79.8 percent of the cases predicted as Certified were actually Certified.
  • The recall score is 0.8947, showing that the model correctly identified a large proportion of actual Certified applications.
  • The F1 score is 0.8438, which reflects a strong balance between precision and recall on the training data.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 2,955 Denied cases and 9,744 Certified cases. However, it misclassified 2,461 Denied cases as Certified and 1,147 Certified cases as Denied.
  • The model is better at identifying Certified cases than Denied cases, as shown by the higher number of correctly predicted Certified records.
  • Compared to an untuned Random Forest model that showed perfect training performance, this tuned model has a more controlled training score. This suggests that hyperparameter tuning reduced overfitting and made the model less memorized on the training data.
  • Overall, the tuned Random Forest Classifier shows stable training performance with strong recall and F1 score, making it a better candidate than the highly overfitted untuned Random Forest model.

Evaluation of model performance on the Validation Set¶

In [67]:
# Evaluating tuned Random Forest Classifier on validation data
random_forest_classifier_tuned_validation_perf = model_performance_evaluation(random_forest_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

random_forest_classifier_tuned_validation_perf
Out[67]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.745401 0.770465 0.881381 0.822199
In [68]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned Random Forest Classifier on validation data
confusion_matrix(random_forest_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

Observations:

  • The tuned Random Forest Classifier achieved a validation accuracy of 0.7442, meaning the model correctly classified around 74.4 percent of the validation records.
  • The precision score is 0.7731, which indicates that around 77.3 percent of the cases predicted as Certified were actually Certified.
  • The recall score is 0.8733, showing that the model correctly identified most of the actual Certified applications.
  • The F1 score is 0.8201, which shows a strong balance between precision and recall on unseen validation data.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 656 Denied cases and 2,378 Certified cases. However, it misclassified 698 Denied cases as Certified and 345 Certified cases as Denied.
  • The model performs better in identifying Certified cases compared to Denied cases, as the number of correctly predicted Certified records is much higher than the correctly predicted Denied records.
  • Compared with the training performance, the validation scores are slightly lower but still close. This suggests that the tuned Random Forest model generalizes reasonably well and does not show severe overfitting.
  • Overall, the tuned Random Forest Classifier provides stable validation performance with high recall and a strong F1 score, making it a reliable model for identifying Certified applications.

Hyperparameter Tuning of the AdaBoost Classifier¶

In [69]:
# Measuring execution time for AdaBoost tuning
%%time

# Initializing AdaBoost Classifier
adaboost_classifier_tuned = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state = 42)

# Defining hyperparameter search space
param_grid = {
    'n_estimators': np.arange(50, 301, 50),
    'learning_rate': np.arange(0.01, 1.01, 0.1),
    'estimator': [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=42),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=2, random_state=42),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=42)]
}

# Creating F1 score as the evaluation metric
acc_scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

# Setting up RandomizedSearchCV for hyperparameter tuning
random_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
    adaboost_classifier_tuned,
    param_grid,
    scoring = acc_scorer,
    cv = 5,
    n_jobs = -1,
    verbose = 2,
    n_iter = 30
)

# Fitting randomized search on training data
random_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Selecting the best tuned AdaBoost Classifier
adaboost_classifier_tuned = random_search.best_estimator_

# Training the best model on training data
adaboost_classifier_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)

Evaluation of model performance on the Training Set¶

In [70]:
# Evaluating tuned AdaBoost Classifier on training data
adaboost_classifier_tuned_training_perf = model_performance_evaluation(adaboost_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

adaboost_classifier_tuned_training_perf
Out[70]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.751763 0.779969 0.875218 0.824853
In [71]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned AdaBoost Classifier on training data
confusion_matrix(adaboost_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

Observations:

  • The tuned AdaBoost Classifier achieved a training accuracy of 0.7518, indicating that the model correctly classified around 75.2 percent of the training records.
  • The precision score is 0.7800, which means that among the records predicted as Certified, around 78.0 percent were actually Certified.
  • The recall score is 0.8752, showing that the model correctly identified a large proportion of actual Certified applications.
  • The F1 score is 0.8249, which indicates a strong balance between precision and recall on the training data.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 2,727 Denied cases and 9,532 Certified cases. However, it misclassified 2,689 Denied cases as Certified and 1,359 Certified cases as Denied.
  • The model is better at identifying Certified applications than Denied applications, as the number of correctly predicted Certified cases is much higher than the correctly predicted Denied cases.
  • Overall, the tuned AdaBoost Classifier shows balanced training performance with high recall, meaning it is effective in capturing Certified cases while maintaining reasonable precision.

Evaluation of model performance on the Validation Set¶

In [72]:
# Evaluating tuned AdaBoost Classifier on validation data
adaboost_classifier_tuned_validation_perf = model_performance_evaluation(adaboost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

adaboost_classifier_tuned_validation_perf
Out[72]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.741967 0.770301 0.874403 0.819057
In [73]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned AdaBoost Classifier on validation data
confusion_matrix(adaboost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

Observations:

  • The tuned AdaBoost Classifier achieved a validation accuracy of 0.7420, meaning it correctly classified around 74.2 percent of the validation records.
  • The precision score is 0.7703, indicating that when the model predicted an application as Certified, around 77.0 percent of those predictions were correct.
  • The recall score is 0.8744, showing that the model successfully identified most of the actual Certified applications in the validation set. The F1 score is 0.8191, which reflects a strong balance between precision and recall on unseen validation data.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 644 Denied cases and 2,381 Certified cases. However, it misclassified 710 Denied cases as Certified and 342 Certified cases as Denied.
  • The model performs better in identifying Certified cases than Denied cases, as the number of true positives is high while the number of missed Certified cases is relatively low.
  • Compared with its training performance, the validation results are very close, which indicates that the tuned AdaBoost model generalizes well and does not show major overfitting.

Hyperparameter Tuning of the Gradient Boosting Classifier¶

In [74]:
# Measuring execution time for Gradient Boosting tuning
%%time

# Initializing Gradient Boosting Classifier
gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state = 42)

# Defining hyperparameter search space
param_grid = {
    'n_estimators': np.arange(100, 501, 100),
    'learning_rate': np.arange(0.01, 0.21, 0.05),
    'max_depth': np.arange(2, 8, 1),
    'min_samples_split': np.arange(2, 21, 2),
    'min_samples_leaf': np.arange(1, 11, 2),
    'subsample': np.arange(0.6, 1.01, 0.1),
    'max_features': ['sqrt', 'log2', None]
}

# Creating F1 score as the evaluation metric
acc_scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

# Setting up RandomizedSearchCV for hyperparameter tuning
random_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
    gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned,
    param_grid,
    scoring = acc_scorer,
    cv = 5,
    n_jobs = -1,
    verbose = 2,
    n_iter = 30
)

# Fitting randomized search on training data
random_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Selecting the best tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier
gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned = random_search.best_estimator_

# Training the best model on training data
gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)

Fitting 5 folds for each of 30 candidates, totalling 150 fits
CPU times: user 12.4 s, sys: 1.59 s, total: 14 s
Wall time: 12min 31s
Out[74]:
GradientBoostingClassifier(learning_rate=np.float64(0.01),
                           max_depth=np.int64(5), max_features='sqrt',
                           min_samples_leaf=np.int64(9),
                           min_samples_split=np.int64(16),
                           n_estimators=np.int64(300), random_state=42,
                           subsample=np.float64(0.6))

GradientBoostingClassifier(learning_rate=np.float64(0.01),
                           max_depth=np.int64(5), max_features='sqrt',
                           min_samples_leaf=np.int64(9),
                           min_samples_split=np.int64(16),
                           n_estimators=np.int64(300), random_state=42,
                           subsample=np.float64(0.6))

Evaluation of model performance on the Training Set¶

In [75]:
# Evaluating tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier on training data
gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned_training_perf = model_performance_evaluation(gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned_training_perf
Out[75]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.758079 0.779045 0.890276 0.830955
In [76]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier on training data
confusion_matrix(gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

Observations:

  • The tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier achieved a training accuracy of 0.7564, meaning it correctly classified around 75.6 percent of the training records.
  • The precision score is 0.7805, indicating that when the model predicted an application as Certified, around 78.0 percent of those predictions were correct.
  • The recall score is 0.8839, showing that the model correctly identified a large portion of the actual Certified applications in the training data.
  • The F1 score is 0.8290, which indicates a good balance between precision and recall, with stronger performance toward identifying Certified cases.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 2,708 Denied cases and 9,627 Certified cases. However, it misclassified 2,708 Denied cases as Certified and 1,264 Certified cases as Denied.
  • The model is better at detecting Certified cases than Denied cases, as the number of correctly predicted Certified cases is high and the number of missed Certified cases is relatively low.
  • The equal number of true Denied and falsely Certified predictions shows that the model still struggles to separate some Denied applications from Certified applications.
  • Overall, the tuned Gradient Boosting model shows stable training performance and does not appear to overfit severely, but its ability to correctly identify Denied cases can still be improved.

Evaluation of model performance on the Validation Set¶

Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.741722 0.766433 0.882115 0.820215
In [78]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier on validation data
confusion_matrix(gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

Observations:

  • The tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier achieved a validation accuracy of 0.7449, meaning it correctly classified nearly 74.5 percent of the validation records.
  • The precision score is 0.7708, showing that when the model predicted an application as Certified, around 77.1 percent of those predictions were correct.
  • The recall score is 0.8795, which means the model correctly identified most of the actual Certified applications in the validation data.
  • The F1 score is 0.8216, indicating a strong balance between precision and recall on unseen validation data.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 642 Denied cases and 2,395 Certified cases. However, 712 Denied cases were incorrectly predicted as Certified, while 328 Certified cases were incorrectly predicted as Denied.
  • Compared with the training performance, the validation performance is very close, which suggests that the tuned Gradient Boosting model generalizes well and does not show strong overfitting.
  • Overall, the model performs well in identifying Certified applications, but it still has difficulty correctly classifying Denied cases.

Hyperparameter Tuning of the XGBoost Classifier¶

In [79]:
# Measuring execution time for XGBoost tuning
%%time

# Initializing XGBoost Classifier
xgboost_classifier_tuned = XGBClassifier(random_state = 42)

# Defining hyperparameter grid for GridSearchCV
param_grid = {
    'n_estimators': np.arange(100, 301, 100),
    'learning_rate': np.arange(0.05, 0.16, 0.05),
    'max_depth': np.arange(3, 6, 1),
    'subsample': np.arange(0.8, 1.01, 0.1),
    'colsample_bytree': np.arange(0.8, 1.01, 0.1),
    'scale_pos_weight': [1, 2, 3]
}

# Creating F1 score as the evaluation metric
acc_scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

# Setting up GridSearchCV for exhaustive hyperparameter tuning
grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    xgboost_classifier_tuned,
    param_grid,
    scoring = acc_scorer,
    cv =5,
    n_jobs = -1,
    verbose =2
)

# Fitting grid search on training data
grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Selecting the best tuned XGBoost Classifier
xgboost_classifier_tuned = grid_search.best_estimator_

# Training the best model on training data
xgboost_classifier_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)

Evaluation of model performance on the Training Set¶

In [80]:
# Evaluating tuned XGBoost Classifier on training data
xgboost_classifier_tuned_training_perf = model_performance_evaluation(xgboost_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

xgboost_classifier_tuned_training_perf
Out[80]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.764518 0.788762 0.884216 0.833766
In [81]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned XGBoost Classifier on training data
confusion_matrix(xgboost_classifier_tuned, X_train, y_train)

Observations:

  • The tuned XGBoost Classifier achieved a training accuracy of 0.7645, indicating that the model correctly classified around 76.45 percent of the training records.
  • The precision score is 0.7888, meaning that when the model predicted an application as Certified, nearly 78.88 percent of those predictions were correct.
  • The recall score is 0.8842, which shows that the model successfully identified most of the actual Certified applications in the training data.
  • The F1 score is 0.8338, indicating a strong balance between precision and recall, with the model performing especially well in identifying the Certified class.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 2,837 Denied cases and 9,630 Certified cases. However, 2,579 Denied cases were incorrectly predicted as Certified, and 1,261 Certified cases were incorrectly predicted as Denied.
  • The model is more effective at identifying Certified applications than Denied applications, as the number of correctly predicted Certified cases is much higher.
  • Compared with untuned XGBoost, the tuned model shows lower training performance, which is a positive sign because hyperparameter tuning has reduced overfitting and made the model less complex.
  • Overall, the tuned XGBoost model provides a good balance between accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, but it still needs improvement in correctly identifying Denied cases.

Evaluation of model performance on the Validation Set¶

In [82]:
# Evaluating tuned XGBoost Classifier on validation data
xgboost_classifier_tuned_validation_perf = model_performance_evaluation(xgboost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

xgboost_classifier_tuned_validation_perf
Out[82]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.747363 0.776003 0.874036 0.822107
In [83]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for tuned XGBoost Classifier on validation data
confusion_matrix(xgboost_classifier_tuned, X_val, y_val)

Observations:

  • The tuned XGBoost Classifier achieved a validation accuracy of 0.7474, meaning the model correctly classified around 74.74 percent of validation records.
  • The precision score is 0.7760, which indicates that when the model predicted an application as Certified, around 77.60 percent of those predictions were correct.
  • The recall score is 0.8740, showing that the model successfully identified most of the actual Certified applications in the validation set.
  • The F1 score is 0.8221, which reflects a strong balance between precision and recall and shows that the model performs well on unseen validation data.
  • From the confusion matrix, the model correctly classified 667 Denied cases and 2,380 Certified cases. However, 687 Denied cases were incorrectly predicted as Certified, while 343 Certified cases were incorrectly predicted as Denied.
  • The model performs better in identifying Certified applications than Denied applications, as the recall for the Certified class is high and the number of correctly predicted Certified cases is much larger.
  • Compared with the training performance, the validation performance is very close, which indicates that the tuned XGBoost model generalizes well and does not show strong overfitting.
  • Overall, the tuned XGBoost Classifier is a reliable model with strong recall and F1 score, but it still has room for improvement in reducing false positives for the Denied class.

Comparison of the model performance and final model selection¶

Comparison of Training model performance¶

In [84]:
# Comparing training performance of all tuned models
training_model_comparison_df = pd.concat([
    bagging_classifier_tuned_training_perf.T,
    random_forest_classifier_tuned_training_perf.T,
    adaboost_classifier_tuned_training_perf.T,
    gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned_training_perf.T,
    xgboost_classifier_tuned_training_perf.T
], axis = 1)

# Assigning model names as column labels
training_model_comparison_df.columns = [
    'Bagging Classifier',
    'Random Forest Classifier',
    'Adaboost Classifier',
    'Gradient Boosting Classifier',
    'XGBoost Classifier'
]

# Displaying training performance comparison
training_model_comparison_df.T
Out[84]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
Bagging Classifier 0.832293 0.796542 0.892572 0.841827
Random Forest Classifier 0.762985 0.783672 0.891103 0.833942
Adaboost Classifier 0.751763 0.779969 0.875218 0.824853
Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.758079 0.779045 0.890276 0.830955
XGBoost Classifier 0.764518 0.788762 0.884216 0.833766

Observations:

  • The tuned Bagging Classifier shows the highest training accuracy of 0.8705, the highest recall of 0.9776, and the highest F1 score of 0.8831 among all tuned models.
  • The high recall of the Bagging Classifier indicates that it is very effective at identifying Certified cases in the training data. However, the large gap between its training and validation performance suggests some overfitting.
  • The tuned Random Forest Classifier has a training accuracy of 0.7787 and an F1 score of 0.8438. Its performance is moderate on the training set, but it generalizes better than the Bagging Classifier because its validation score remains close to its training score.
  • The tuned AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost classifiers show similar training performance, with F1 scores ranging from 0.8249 to 0.8338. This indicates stable learning without extreme overfitting.
  • Among the boosting models, XGBoost gives the best training performance with an accuracy of 0.7645, precision of 0.7888, recall of 0.8842, and F1 score of 0.8338.
  • Overall, the Bagging Classifier performs best on the training data, but its very high recall and F1 score should be interpreted carefully because it may have learned the training patterns too strongly. XGBoost and Gradient Boosting appear more balanced and reliable when considering both training stability and validation performance.

Comparison of Validation model performance¶

In [85]:
# Comparing validation performance of all tuned models
training_model_comparison_df = pd.concat([
    bagging_classifier_tuned_validation_perf.T,
    random_forest_classifier_tuned_validation_perf.T,
    adaboost_classifier_tuned_validation_perf.T,
    gradient_boosting_classifier_tuned_validation_perf.T,
    xgboost_classifier_tuned_validation_perf.T
], axis = 1)

# Assigning model names as column labels
training_model_comparison_df.columns = [
    'Bagging Classifier',
    'Random Forest Classifier',
    'Adaboost Classifier',
    'Gradient Boosting Classifier',
    'XGBoost Classifier'
]

# Displaying validation performance comparison
training_model_comparison_df.T
Out[85]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
Bagging Classifier 0.744175 0.773260 0.872934 0.820079
Random Forest Classifier 0.745401 0.770465 0.881381 0.822199
Adaboost Classifier 0.741967 0.770301 0.874403 0.819057
Gradient Boosting Classifier 0.741722 0.766433 0.882115 0.820215
XGBoost Classifier 0.747363 0.776003 0.874036 0.822107

Observations:

  • The tuned XGBoost Classifier gives the best validation performance with an accuracy of 0.7474 and the highest F1 score of 0.8221 among all tuned models.
  • The tuned Gradient Boosting Classifier performs very close to XGBoost, with an accuracy of 0.7449, recall of 0.8795, and F1 score of 0.8216. This indicates that both boosting models generalize well on unseen validation data.
  • The tuned Bagging Classifier has the highest validation recall of 0.9199, meaning it identifies the maximum number of Certified cases. However, its precision is the lowest at 0.7407, which shows that it also produces more false positives.
  • The tuned Random Forest Classifier shows balanced validation performance with accuracy of 0.7442, precision of 0.7731, recall of 0.8733, and F1 score of 0.8201.
  • The tuned AdaBoost Classifier performs slightly lower than the other tuned models, with an F1 score of 0.8191, but the difference is very small.
  • Overall, XGBoost can be considered the best tuned model based on validation F1 score, while Gradient Boosting is a very close alternative. Bagging is useful when maximizing recall is the main goal, but it sacrifices precision.

Test Set evaluation of the best model¶

In [86]:
# Evaluating final tuned XGBoost Classifier on test data
xgboost_classifier_tuned_test_perf = model_performance_evaluation(xgboost_classifier_tuned, X_test, y_test)

xgboost_classifier_tuned_test_perf
Out[86]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score
0 0.742543 0.771547 0.87309 0.819184
In [87]:
# Plotting confusion matrix for final tuned XGBoost Classifier on test data
confusion_matrix(xgboost_classifier_tuned, X_test, y_test)

Observations:

  • The final tuned XGBoost Classifier achieves a test accuracy of 0.7425, precision of 0.7715, recall of 0.8731, and F1 score of 0.8192.
  • The test F1 score is very close to the validation F1 score of 0.8221, which shows that the model generalizes well on unseen data and does not show major overfitting.
  • The recall value of 0.8731 indicates that the model is strong at identifying the Certified class, correctly predicting 2972 Certified cases out of 3404 actual Certified cases.
  • The precision value of 0.7715 shows that among all cases predicted as Certified, a reasonable proportion are actually Certified, but there are still 880 Denied cases incorrectly classified as Certified.
  • The confusion matrix shows 812 true negatives, 2972 true positives, 880 false positives, and 432 false negatives. This means the model performs better at detecting Certified cases than Denied cases.
  • Overall, the tuned XGBoost Classifier is a reliable final model because its test performance is consistent with validation performance and it maintains a good balance between precision, recall, and F1 score.

Feature Importances of the best model¶

In [88]:
# Extracting feature importance scores from the tuned XGBoost model
feature_importances = pd.Series(xgboost_classifier_tuned.feature_importances_, index=X_train.columns)

# Sorting features by importance in descending order
feature_importances_sorted = feature_importances.sort_values(ascending=False)

# Plotting feature importance of all input variables
plt.figure(figsize = (10, 6))
sns.barplot(x = feature_importances_sorted.values, y = feature_importances_sorted.index, color = 'violet')
plt.title('Feature Importance')
plt.xlabel('Importance')
plt.ylabel('Features')
plt.show()

Observations:

  • The most influential feature in the final tuned XGBoost model is education_of_employee_High School, with an importance score of around 0.31, making it the strongest predictor by a large margin.
  • has_job_experience_Y is the second most important feature, indicating that prior job experience plays a significant role in predicting visa certification outcomes.
  • Education related features such as Master's, Doctorate, and High School appear among the top predictors, showing that employee education level strongly influences the model’s decisions.
  • Wage related variables such as unit_of_wage_Year, prevailing_wage, unit_of_wage_Week, and unit_of_wage_Month contribute to the prediction, but their influence is lower compared to education and job experience.
  • Geographic features such as employment region and continent also contribute to the model, suggesting that location based patterns have some impact on certification outcomes.
  • Overall, the model relies most heavily on education level, job experience, wage structure, and regional factors, with education_of_employee_High School dominating the prediction process.

Actionable insights, recommendations & conclusion¶

Insights

  • The project focused on predicting visa application outcomes by building and comparing multiple classification models. Since the target variable had an imbalance between certified and denied applications, class balancing techniques were applied before model training. The original training data had more certified applications than denied applications, which could have biased the models toward predicting the majority class.
  • Both oversampling and undersampling were tested to handle this imbalance. Oversampling created a balanced training dataset by increasing the minority class, while undersampling balanced the data by reducing the majority class. Based on the model results, oversampling performed better overall because it improved recall and F1 score without removing useful information from the training data. Undersampling helped balance the classes, but it reduced the training size from the original dataset and caused some loss of information, which affected model performance.
  • Before hyperparameter tuning, tree based models such as Bagging and Random Forest showed strong signs of overfitting. For example, Random Forest achieved perfect training performance but had a much lower validation score. This indicates that the model learned the training data too closely and was not generalizing as well to unseen data. Boosting models such as AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost showed more balanced performance and handled the classification task more effectively.
  • After hyperparameter tuning, the overall performance became more stable across models. The tuned models showed smaller gaps between training and validation performance, which means tuning helped control overfitting. Among all tuned models, XGBoost performed the best on validation data, with an accuracy of 0.7474, precision of 0.7760, recall of 0.8740, and F1 score of 0.8221. Since F1 score balances precision and recall, XGBoost was selected as the final model.
  • The final tuned XGBoost model was evaluated on the test set and achieved an accuracy of 0.7425, precision of 0.7715, recall of 0.8731, and F1 score of 0.8192. These results are very close to the validation performance, showing that the model generalizes well and is not heavily overfitted. The model performed especially well in recall, meaning it was effective at identifying certified applications. However, the precision value shows that some denied applications were still incorrectly predicted as certified.
  • The confusion matrix supports this observation. The model correctly classified a large number of certified applications, but it also produced some false positives by predicting certification for denied cases. This means the model is strong at capturing positive cases but can still be improved in separating denied applications more accurately.
  • The feature importance analysis showed that education level, especially education_of_employee_High School, was the most influential feature in the model. Job experience, wage unit, region of employment, continent, prevailing wage, full time position status, and job training requirement also contributed to the prediction. This suggests that both applicant characteristics and employment related factors play an important role in visa certification outcomes.

Recommendations

  • The tuned XGBoost Classifier should be selected as the final model because it achieved the best validation F1 score and maintained stable performance on the test data.
  • Oversampling should be preferred over undersampling because it improved model performance while preserving all useful training information. Undersampling reduced the dataset size and may have removed important patterns from the majority class.
  • EasyVisa should use this model as an early screening and decision support tool to identify applications with a higher probability of certification or denial. This can help prioritize cases, reduce manual review time, and improve operational efficiency.
  • Applications predicted as highly likely to be certified can be fast tracked for standard processing, while applications with lower certification probability can be flagged for additional document review, expert consultation, or applicant guidance before submission.
  • EasyVisa should pay close attention to key drivers such as education level, prior job experience, prevailing wage, wage unit, full time position status, and region of employment. These factors can help consultants explain application risks more clearly to clients.
  • For applicants with weaker profiles, EasyVisa can provide targeted recommendations such as improving documentation quality, validating wage information, reviewing job requirements, or ensuring that the role and applicant qualifications are aligned.
  • Since the model has high recall but moderate precision, EasyVisa should avoid using the model as an automatic approval or rejection system. Instead, it should support human reviewers by highlighting risk levels and helping them focus on applications that need closer attention.
  • Threshold tuning should be performed based on EasyVisa’s business priority. If the goal is to capture as many potentially certified applications as possible, the current high recall setup is suitable. If the goal is to reduce false certification predictions, the threshold should be adjusted to improve precision.
  • Model explainability techniques such as SHAP should be used to explain individual predictions. This will make the model more transparent and help EasyVisa consultants communicate prediction reasons to applicants in a practical and understandable way.

Conclusion

  • Overall, the project successfully built a machine learning classification framework to predict visa application outcomes. The workflow included class imbalance handling, model comparison, hyperparameter tuning, final model selection, test evaluation, confusion matrix analysis, and feature importance interpretation.
  • The results showed that balancing the dataset was important for improving classification performance. Oversampling was more effective than undersampling because it preserved training information while improving minority class learning. Initial models showed some overfitting, especially Bagging and Random Forest, but hyperparameter tuning improved the stability of the models.
  • Among all models, the tuned XGBoost Classifier was the best performing model. It achieved the highest validation F1 score and produced consistent test performance, confirming that it generalized well to unseen data. The model was particularly strong in identifying certified applications, although further improvement is needed to reduce false certified predictions.
  • In conclusion, the tuned XGBoost model is a reliable final model for this project. It provides strong predictive performance and also gives useful insights into the factors influencing visa certification outcomes. Future work should focus on threshold optimization, stronger model explainability, and additional feature engineering to further improve prediction quality.
In [90]:
!jupyter nbconvert --to html '/content/drive/MyDrive/Projects/EasyVisa_Predicting Visa Approval Outcomes Using Advanced Machine Learning Techniques /EasyVisa_Predicting Visa Approval Outcomes Using Advanced Machine Learning Techniques.ipynb '

[NbConvertApp] WARNING | pattern '/content/drive/MyDrive/Projects/EasyVisa_Predicting Visa Approval Outcomes Using Advanced Machine Learning Techniques /EasyVisa_Predicting Visa Approval Outcomes Using Advanced Machine Learning Techniques.ipynb ' matched no files
This application is used to convert notebook files (*.ipynb)
        to various other formats.

        WARNING: THE COMMANDLINE INTERFACE MAY CHANGE IN FUTURE RELEASES.

Options
=======
The options below are convenience aliases to configurable class-options,
as listed in the "Equivalent to" description-line of the aliases.
To see all configurable class-options for some <cmd>, use:
    <cmd> --help-all

--debug
    set log level to logging.DEBUG (maximize logging output)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level=10]
--show-config
    Show the application's configuration (human-readable format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config=True]
--show-config-json
    Show the application's configuration (json format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config_json=True]
--generate-config
    generate default config file
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.generate_config=True]
-y
    Answer yes to any questions instead of prompting.
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.answer_yes=True]
--execute
    Execute the notebook prior to export.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.enabled=True]
--allow-errors
    Continue notebook execution even if one of the cells throws an error and include the error message in the cell output (the default behaviour is to abort conversion). This flag is only relevant if '--execute' was specified, too.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.allow_errors=True]
--stdin
    read a single notebook file from stdin. Write the resulting notebook with default basename 'notebook.*'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.from_stdin=True]
--stdout
    Write notebook output to stdout instead of files.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class=StdoutWriter]
--inplace
    Run nbconvert in place, overwriting the existing notebook (only
            relevant when converting to notebook format)
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory=]
--clear-output
    Clear output of current file and save in place,
            overwriting the existing notebook.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --ClearOutputPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--coalesce-streams
    Coalesce consecutive stdout and stderr outputs into one stream (within each cell).
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --CoalesceStreamsPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--no-prompt
    Exclude input and output prompts from converted document.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True]
--no-input
    Exclude input cells and output prompts from converted document.
            This mode is ideal for generating code-free reports.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True]
--allow-chromium-download
    Whether to allow downloading chromium if no suitable version is found on the system.
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.allow_chromium_download=True]
--disable-chromium-sandbox
    Disable chromium security sandbox when converting to PDF..
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.disable_sandbox=True]
--show-input
    Shows code input. This flag is only useful for dejavu users.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input=False]
--embed-images
    Embed the images as base64 dataurls in the output. This flag is only useful for the HTML/WebPDF/Slides exports.
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.embed_images=True]
--sanitize-html
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized..
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html=True]
--log-level=<Enum>
    Set the log level by value or name.
    Choices: any of [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 'DEBUG', 'INFO', 'WARN', 'ERROR', 'CRITICAL']
    Default: 30
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level]
--config=<Unicode>
    Full path of a config file.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.config_file]
--to=<Unicode>
    The export format to be used, either one of the built-in formats
            ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf']
            or a dotted object name that represents the import path for an
            ``Exporter`` class
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.export_format]
--template=<Unicode>
    Name of the template to use
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_name]
--template-file=<Unicode>
    Name of the template file to use
    Default: None
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_file]
--theme=<Unicode>
    Template specific theme(e.g. the name of a JupyterLab CSS theme distributed
    as prebuilt extension for the lab template)
    Default: 'light'
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.theme]
--sanitize_html=<Bool>
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized.This
    should be set to True by nbviewer or similar tools.
    Default: False
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html]
--writer=<DottedObjectName>
    Writer class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: 'FilesWriter'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class]
--post=<DottedOrNone>
    PostProcessor class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.postprocessor_class]
--output=<Unicode>
    Overwrite base name use for output files.
                Supports pattern replacements '{notebook_name}'.
    Default: '{notebook_name}'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.output_base]
--output-dir=<Unicode>
    Directory to write output(s) to. Defaults
                                  to output to the directory of each notebook. To recover
                                  previous default behaviour (outputting to the current
                                  working directory) use . as the flag value.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--FilesWriter.build_directory]
--reveal-prefix=<Unicode>
    The URL prefix for reveal.js (version 3.x).
            This defaults to the reveal CDN, but can be any url pointing to a copy
            of reveal.js.
            For speaker notes to work, this must be a relative path to a local
            copy of reveal.js: e.g., "reveal.js".
            If a relative path is given, it must be a subdirectory of the
            current directory (from which the server is run).
            See the usage documentation
            (https://nbconvert.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#reveal-js-html-slideshow)
            for more details.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--SlidesExporter.reveal_url_prefix]
--nbformat=<Enum>
    The nbformat version to write.
            Use this to downgrade notebooks.
    Choices: any of [1, 2, 3, 4]
    Default: 4
    Equivalent to: [--NotebookExporter.nbformat_version]

Examples
--------

    The simplest way to use nbconvert is

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to html

            Options include ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf'].

            > jupyter nbconvert --to latex mynotebook.ipynb

            Both HTML and LaTeX support multiple output templates. LaTeX includes
            'base', 'article' and 'report'.  HTML includes 'basic', 'lab' and
            'classic'. You can specify the flavor of the format used.

            > jupyter nbconvert --to html --template lab mynotebook.ipynb

            You can also pipe the output to stdout, rather than a file

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --stdout

            PDF is generated via latex

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to pdf

            You can get (and serve) a Reveal.js-powered slideshow

            > jupyter nbconvert myslides.ipynb --to slides --post serve

            Multiple notebooks can be given at the command line in a couple of
            different ways:

            > jupyter nbconvert notebook*.ipynb
            > jupyter nbconvert notebook1.ipynb notebook2.ipynb

            or you can specify the notebooks list in a config file, containing::

                c.NbConvertApp.notebooks = ["my_notebook.ipynb"]

            > jupyter nbconvert --config mycfg.py

To see all available configurables, use `--help-all`.
